serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Korean steamed egg with cheese is approximately 200 g, served in a hot stone pot or ceramic bowl.

nutrient_source.title: Eggs, whole, cooked, scrambled / Cheese, mozzarella, whole milk — composite estimate
nutrient_source.organization: USDA FoodData Central (Foundation Foods / SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for Korean steamed egg with cheese exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA. Nutrient values estimated from USDA FoodData Central using a weighted composite of cooked whole egg (FDC ID 173424, approximately 80% by weight) and whole-milk mozzarella cheese (FDC ID 171242, approximately 20% by weight), reflecting a typical gyeran-jjim recipe with mozzarella topping. Sodium adjusted upward to reflect added salt and broth typical in restaurant preparation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — egg-based and high-protein low-carbohydrate foods
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Korean steamed egg with cheese (gyeran-jjim). GI estimated at 30 based on analogy with cooked whole egg dishes and high-protein, low-carbohydrate egg preparations documented in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008) international GI tables. Carbohydrate content per 100 g is approximately 1.8 g; glycemic load per 200 g serving = (30 × 1.8 × 2) / 100 = 1.1, classified as low GL. GI category: low (GI < 55).

hypertension.guidance: Each 200 g restaurant serving provides approximately 640 mg sodium, representing about 28% of the 2300 mg daily limit recommended for hypertensive individuals; portion control and requesting reduced-salt preparation are advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content and low glycemic index
type2_diabetes.guidance: With only 1.8 g carbohydrate per 100 g and an estimated GI of 30, steamed egg with cheese has a negligible glycemic load and is generally suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, though the saturated fat from mozzarella should be monitored in the context of overall dietary fat intake.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Dietary cholesterol and saturated fat from egg yolk and mozzarella
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: A 200 g serving provides approximately 620 mg dietary cholesterol and 7.6 g saturated fat; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit frequency of consumption and consider egg-white-based preparations to reduce cholesterol and saturated fat load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Steamed Egg with Cheese (치즈 계란찜, Chijeu Gyeran-jjim) is a Korean comfort dish consisting of whole eggs whisked with water or light broth, seasoned with salt and a few drops of sesame oil, then gently steamed in a stone pot or ceramic bowl until the mixture sets into a soft, airy, soufflé-like custard. In this version, melted mozzarella cheese is incorporated or placed on top, adding a creamy, stretchy richness that complements the delicate egg texture. The dish is a popular side dish (banchan) or light main in Korean restaurants, including Korean-style casual dining establishments in Singapore such as Ajumma's. Nutritionally, it is high in complete protein (approximately 10.8 g per 100 g), moderate in fat (9.6 g per 100 g, including 3.8 g saturated fat from egg yolk and mozzarella), and very low in carbohydrates (1.8 g per 100 g), making it suitable for low-carbohydrate dietary patterns. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 30 (low), and the glycemic load per serving is negligible at 1.1. Sodium content is moderate to high in restaurant preparations due to added salt and broth, which is a consideration for individuals managing hypertension. The dish is a good source of vitamin B12, vitamin D, vitamin A, phosphorus, and selenium from the egg and cheese components. It is vegetarian and halal-friendly but not suitable for those with egg or dairy allergies.

confidence_reason: No direct entry for Korean steamed egg with cheese was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite of USDA FoodData Central entries for cooked whole egg and whole-milk mozzarella cheese, which are approved secondary sources. The GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for comparable high-protein, low-carbohydrate egg dishes. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central (Foundation Foods / SR Legacy): cooked whole egg (FDC ID 173424, ~80% weight fraction) and whole-milk mozzarella (FDC ID 171242, ~20% weight fraction). Sodium is adjusted upward to 320 mg/100 g to reflect typical restaurant-level salting and broth addition. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero — eggs and mozzarella contain no dietary fibre by composition). vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero — eggs and mozzarella contain no vitamin C). monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from the same USDA composite and are not independently verified for this specific restaurant preparation. GI of 30 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for gyeran-jjim with cheese was located in the University of Sydney GI Database as of the accessed date. Price data (SGD 10.50) preserved from field survey data input record for reference only; not used as a nutritional source. FoodOn and FoodEx2 codes are closest-parent matches; no exact ontology code for Korean steamed egg with cheese exists in the reviewed ontologies.
